note_data.title: Transport in plants
keyPoints: Plant cells contain unique organelles not found in animal cells: vacuole, cell wall, chloroplasts, and plasmodesmata
The vacuole is surrounded by a membrane called the tonoplast and contains mineral salts, sugars, amino acids, waste substances and pigments
The cell wall is made of cellulose microfibrils and strengthens the cell while preventing bursting due to osmosis
Chloroplasts are the site of photosynthesis and contain thylakoid membranes stacked to form grana, linked by lamellae
Plasmodesmata are small channels through cell walls that allow communication between adjoining plant cells
The vacuole provides support through turgidity, acts as temporary food store, and colours cells to attract pollinating insects
Chloroplasts contain small circular DNA and 70S ribosomes, similar to mitochondria

definitions.definition: The membrane surrounding the vacuole in plant cells
The structural material that makes up plant cell walls
Stacks of thylakoid membranes found in chloroplasts
Structures that link grana together in chloroplasts
Small channels that pass through cell walls allowing communication between adjoining plant cells

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of events or features without explaining why
State similarities and differences between xylem and phloem transport

modelAnswer.question: Explain how the cohesion-tension theory accounts for the movement of water up a tall tree.
modelAnswer.answer: Transpiration from leaves creates a water potential gradient. Water evaporates from mesophyll cells and diffuses out through stomata. This creates tension in the xylem vessels. Water molecules cohere to each other due to hydrogen bonding between water molecules. This cohesion allows water to be pulled up as a continuous column. The adhesion of water molecules to xylem walls prevents the water column from breaking. Root pressure may assist but is not essential for water movement in tall trees.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Transpiration creates water potential gradient
Mark 3: Water molecules cohere due to hydrogen bonding
Mark 4: Water pulled up as continuous column
Mark 6: Root pressure assists but not essential

commonMistakes: Confusing the tonoplast with the cell wall - the tonoplast is specifically the vacuole membrane
Thinking all plant cells have the same organelles as animal cells - plant cells have unique structures
Forgetting that chloroplasts have their own DNA and ribosomes like mitochondria
examinerTips: Remember the specific functions of plant-only organelles: vacuole for support and storage, cell wall for strength, chloroplasts for photosynthesis
Focus on the structural details of chloroplasts including thylakoids, grana and lamellae for photosynthesis questions
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Cross-section of plant stem showing xylem vessels (thick lignified walls, no end walls, dead) and phloem (sieve tube elements with sieve plates, companion cells with dense cytoplasm). Label showing direction of transport: xylem upward only, phloem bidirectional.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain the structure and function of xylem vessels and relate this to water transport
N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — required identification of the tissue responsible for translocation of sucrose in plants
M/J 2022 P2 Q3(c) (4 marks) — students had to describe the transpiration stream and explain factors affecting transpiration rate
N 2023 P2 Q5 (7 marks) — comprehensive question on comparing transport in xylem and phloem, including adaptations of vessel elements
M/J 2021 P2 Q2(b) (3 marks) — asked students to explain why transport systems are necessary in large plants but not in small organisms

workedExample.question: Explain how the structure of xylem vessels is adapted for the transport of water and mineral salts. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that xylem vessels are made of dead cells with no cytoplasm or organelles
Step 2: Explain that cell walls are thickened with lignin for structural support
Step 3: Describe that end walls break down to form continuous tubes
Step 4: Link each structural feature to its function in water transport
workedExample.answer: Xylem vessels are composed of dead cells with no cytoplasm, nucleus or other organelles, which means there is no obstruction to water flow. The cell walls are thickened with lignin, providing structural support to withstand the negative pressure created during transpiration. The end walls between adjacent cells break down completely, forming continuous hollow tubes that allow unimpeded water movement. The narrow diameter of vessels creates adhesive forces between water molecules and vessel walls, helping maintain the water column during transport.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for dead cells/no cytoplasm providing unobstructed flow; 1 mark for lignified walls providing support/preventing collapse; 1 mark for end walls breaking down forming continuous tubes; 1 mark for narrow diameter creating adhesive forces or any other valid adaptation

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused xylem and phloem functions' — always specify that xylem transports water and minerals UP the plant, while phloem transports sucrose and amino acids in both directions

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often failed to link structure to function in transport questions' — when describing vessel structure, always explain WHY each feature helps with transport, don't just list structures

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently gave vague answers about transpiration factors' — be specific about HOW temperature, humidity, wind speed and light intensity affect transpiration rate, not just that they do
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a cross-section of a plant stem showing xylem and phloem arrangement. The diagram should include: circular cross-section with epidermis (outer layer), cortex (large cells), vascular bundles arranged in a ring containing xylem (toward center, with thick-walled vessels) and phloem (toward outside, with thin-walled sieve tubes and companion cells), and central pith. Label all tissues clearly and indicate direction of transport with arrows - water up through xylem, organic substances in both directions through phloem. Include at least 4-5 xylem vessels drawn as circles with thick walls, and 3-4 phloem sieve tubes as thinner circles.
note_data.quickRecap: Plant cells contain specialized organelles for their unique functions. The vacuole provides structural support and storage, while the cellulose cell wall prevents osmotic bursting. Chloroplasts carry out photosynthesis using their internal thylakoid membrane system organized into grana. Plasmodesmata enable cell-to-cell communication through the cell wall.
